era_context: Roman Empire under Tiberius (14–37 CE). Early Christian movement in Judea. Han Dynasty in China (206 BCE–9 CE) in its later phase. Parthian Empire under Artabanus II (r. 10–40 CE). Jewish Second Temple period in Judea under Roman prefectural rule.

events.title: Crucifixion of Jesus (traditional Christian dating)
events.description: Christian tradition places the crucifixion of Jesus of Nazareth at approximately 30–33 CE, with 33 CE being a common scholarly estimate. According to the Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John), Jesus was arrested by Jewish authorities, tried before the Roman prefect Pontius Pilate, and executed by crucifixion during the Passover festival. The Gospels were written 40–70 years after the events and contain significant theological interpretation. No contemporary Roman or Jewish source mentions Jesus's crucifixion. The exact year is uncertain; scholars debate whether it occurred in 30, 33, or another year in the late 20s or early 30s CE. The crucifixion is foundational to Christianity and had immense long-term historical impact, but its immediate impact on Roman or Jewish society is not documented in contemporary sources.
key_figures: Pontius Pilate (Roman prefect of Judea, r. 26–36 CE)

sources.name: Tacitus, Annals 15.44 (mentions Jesus's execution under Pilate)

events.certainty_note: The crucifixion is attested in early Christian sources (Paul's letters, Gospels) and mentioned in passing by Tacitus (writing ~100 CE). However, the precise year is not given in any contemporary document. Scholars estimate 30–33 CE based on Gospel chronology, astronomical calculations (Passover dates), and Pilate's tenure (26–36 CE). The year 33 CE is a conventional estimate, not a confirmed historical fact. Some scholars argue for 30 CE; others for 29 or 34 CE.

events.title: Pontius Pilate's tenure as Roman prefect of Judea (ongoing)
events.description: Pontius Pilate served as Roman prefect (praefectus) of Judea from 26 to 36 CE. The year 33 CE falls in the middle of his tenure. Pilate is known from Roman sources (Tacitus, Cassius Dio) and Jewish sources (Philo, Josephus) as a harsh administrator who provoked Jewish opposition through his disregard for Jewish religious sensibilities and his use of force to suppress dissent. His most famous act in Christian tradition is the authorization of Jesus's crucifixion, though this is not mentioned in Roman sources. Pilate's governorship was marked by several incidents of violence against Jewish populations, including the massacre of Samaritans in 36 CE, which led to his recall to Rome.

sources.name: Philo of Alexandria, Embassy to Gaius

events.certainty_note: Pilate's tenure as prefect (26–36 CE) is confirmed by the Pilate Stone (an inscription found in Caesarea Maritima) and by multiple ancient sources. His reputation for harshness is attested in Jewish and Roman sources. The year 33 CE falls within his governorship.
cross_references: 33-001 (Crucifixion of Jesus, traditionally dated to Pilate's tenure)
36-001 (Pilate's recall to Rome following the Samaritan massacre)

events.title: Han Dynasty: Emperor Chengdi's reign (if year is 33 BCE)
events.description: If the intended year is 33 BCE (not 33 CE), Emperor Chengdi of Han (r. 33–7 BCE) ascended to the throne. Chengdi was the son of Emperor Yuandi and inherited a dynasty in decline. His reign was marked by the growing influence of the imperial consort families, particularly the Wang family, and by military pressure from the Xiongnu. Chengdi's reign saw a shift in power dynamics at court, with the emperor increasingly dominated by his consorts' families. This period laid the groundwork for the Wang family's eventual usurpation in 9 CE under Wang Mang.

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: If 33 BCE is intended, Chengdi's accession to the throne is confirmed in Han Shu. The date is certain.
cross_references: 9-001 (Wang Mang's usurpation and founding of Xin Dynasty)

disconfirming_evidence: The crucifixion of Jesus is traditionally dated to 33 CE, but this is a scholarly estimate with no contemporary confirmation. The Gospels were written 40–70 years after the events and contain significant theological interpretation. Some scholars argue for 30 CE or other years in the late 20s or early 30s CE. The exact year cannot be determined from surviving sources. Additionally, the Gospels' account of Pilate's role in the crucifixion is not corroborated by Roman sources (Tacitus, Cassius Dio, Josephus), which mention Pilate's harshness but do not mention Jesus. The Gospel accounts also contain historical inaccuracies (e.g., the census under Quirinius, the trial procedures) that complicate their reliability as historical sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 33 CE is heavily shaped by Christian sources (Gospels, Paul's letters) written decades after the events and with theological rather than historical purposes. Roman sources (Tacitus, Cassius Dio) provide minimal information about Judea in this period and do not mention Jesus's crucifixion. Jewish sources (Philo, Josephus) were written after 70 CE and reflect post-destruction perspectives. The year 33 CE is significant in Christian tradition but its historical significance in the broader context of the Roman Empire or Jewish society is not documented in contemporary sources. The dating of the crucifixion to 33 CE is a scholarly convention, not a historical fact.

graph_edges.note: The crucifixion of Jesus initiated the Christian movement, leading to Paul's conversion and the spread of Christianity
Pilate's tenure as prefect (33 CE) led to his recall in 36 CE following the Samaritan massacre
